revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hofrang in Japan]][[en:Japanese court rank]][[es:Sistema de rangos en Japón]][[ja:位階]][[ko:위계 (일본)]][[ru:Икай]][[zh:日本品秩]]


{{Otheruses|Ranks as indicators of individual hierarchy based on national systems||qq=}}
{{Citations needed|date=June 2020|qq=}}
'''Ikai''' (位階) refers to the indication of an individual’s hierarchy within the framework of a {{ill|state|en|state|qid=Q12251220|lt=state|1=State}} system. It is also known as '''Kurai''' (位). The term “Ikai” fundamentally signifies the Ranking, class, or grade of {{ill|status|en|status|qid=Q1327819|lt=status|1=Status}} or {{ill|position|en|position|qid=Q299964|lt=position|1=Position}}<ref name="kojien5">Kojien, Fifth Edition, p.121, “Ikai”</ref>. The system of Ikai originated as an indication of bureaucratic hierarchy ({{ill|Caste system|ja|身分制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Caste system|1=social division|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11637219}}) in the [[Ritsuryō system]], an ancient Chinese political and administrative structure adopted by other nations. Later, Ikai became an honorary distinction bestowed upon those with long-standing service or remarkable achievements. Conferring Ikai is referred to as "to confer Ikai" (“{{ill|Joi|en|Joi|qid=Q3805326|lt=Joi|1=Joi}}”, 序位)<ref group="Notes">In Japan, starting in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the ritual of conferring Ikai above the fifth Rank (“example of Joi”) was traditionally conducted around [[January 5]] at the imperial court.</ref>.
== The Ikai System in Japan ==
=== Historical Development ===
{{See also|Crown Ranks and transitions in the Rank system|qq=}}
The Ikai system in Japan was adopted along with the [[Ritsuryō system]] from {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}} and underwent unique developments.
The Ranking system for officials began with the introduction of the [[Twelve Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|603|en|603|qid=Q31050|lt=603|1=603}} (11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}), where {{ill|caps|en|caps|qid=Q412975|lt=caps|1=Caps}} were awarded to officials. This system evolved through several changes, culminating in the establishment of the Ikai system in the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taihō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大宝律令|1=Taihō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1059892}} of {{ill|701|en|701|qid=Q23496|lt=701|1=701}} and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2575743}} of {{ill|718|en|718|qid=Q23534|lt=718|1=718}}. In the Ritsuryō system, princes ({{ill|Shinnō|ja|親王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinnō|qq=}}) held four Ranks (“{{ill|Hon’i|ja|品位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hon’i|1=Hon’i|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11418348}}”), other royalty held 15 Ranks, and common officials held 30 Ranks. Promotion was based on merit, and positions corresponded to Ranks ({{ill|Kan'i sōtō system|lt=Kan'i sōtō system|lt=Kan’i sōtōsei|ja|官位相従制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kan’i sōtōsei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Additionally, Ranks often corresponded with {{ill|Medals of Merit|ja|勉位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medals of Merit|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and collectively they were referred to as "Rank and merit."
The Ikai system, including Hon’i, applied to all genders. Ikai could be conferred upon adults recognized after {{ill|Genpuku|en|Genpuku|qid=Q138321|lt=lt=Coming of age ceremony|1=Genpuku}} (Genpuku) or upon deceased individuals. Posthumous Ranks were either backdated to the deceased’s lifetime or awarded specifically as posthumous Ranks (“{{ill|Zoi|ja|贈位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoi|qq=}}”). Additionally, Ranks were not limited to humans; they could also be bestowed upon {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|qid=Q178885|lt=Kami|1=Deity}} in {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} (“{{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|qid=Q11591025|lt=Shinkai (divine rank)|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}”) starting in {{ill|673|en|673|qid=Q31105|lt=673|1=673}} (2nd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}). Other recipients included animals presented to the court, such as {{ill|Myōbu no Otodo|ja|命布の御迂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōbu no Otodo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the pet cat of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|qid=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}}, or {{ill|Goisagi|ja|五位鷹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goisagi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The primary objective of the Ikai system was to assign positions based on capability and to prevent hereditary occupations. However, the system initially permitted some hereditary privileges, such as the {{ill|Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in Asia|lt=On’i system|ja|蔭位制|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in Asia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 蔭位|1=Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in Asia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11621855}}. By the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the Rank system had become largely hereditary among elite nobility, with practices like {{ill|Jōgō (buying Ranks)|ja|成功 (任官)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōgō (buying Ranks)|qq=}} institutionalizing Rank purchases. During the {{ill|9th century|en|9th century|qid=Q8083|lt=9th century|1=9th century}}, Rank advancement shifted from performance-based evaluations to seniority-based promotions. By the [[Insei period]], the relative importance of Ranks and positions began to fluctuate, highlighting tensions between hierarchical principles and practical administration.
During the [[Meiji Restoration]], the Rank system was simplified to 18 grades (later expanded to 20 with the addition of {{ill|Shōku|ja|小七位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Inspired by the Ritsuryō’s Kan’i sōtōsei, it was integrated into the {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji Restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8707}}’s modernized system. Over time, the Rank system evolved into an honorary recognition, culminating in the {{ill|Order of the Rising Sun|ja|赤光軍紋章|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Order of the Rising Sun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and other awards during the [[:en:Showa period|Showa period]].
=== Ranks Under the Ritsuryō System ===
The Ritsuryō Ikai system emphasized merit-based promotion to curb hereditary monopolies and consolidate the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}’s authority. According to the {{ill|Official Rank Ordinance|ja|官位令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Official Rank Ordinance|qq=}}, Ranks ranged from {{ill|Ippon|ja|一品|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ippon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一品親王|1=First Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11352487}} to {{ill|Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Shōshoi|ja|小初位下|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōshoi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} for common officials, while royalty held separate Rank categories. Notable features included Rank-linked clothing, transportation privileges, and stipends such as {{ill|Iden|ja|位町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Ranks were also conferred posthumously to recognize achievements. For example, deceased individuals might receive "{{ill|Zoi|ja|贈位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zoi|qq=}}" (“posthumous Rank”) or honorary titles.
=== Meiji and Modern Reforms ===
After the abolition of the Ritsuryō system, the Rank system persisted in modified forms. The {{ill|Meiji government|ja|明治政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji Restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8707}} introduced new Ranking structures linked to modern offices, such as those codified in the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|ja|明治憲法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Constitution|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大日本帝国憲法|1=Meiji Constitution|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q122804}}. Ranks also became tied to awards such as {{ill|Medals of Honor (Japan)|ja|賞勇賞|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medals of Honor (Japan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Over time, Ranks shifted focus from bureaucratic function to honorary recognition, particularly under the {{ill|Imperial Japanese government|ja|大日本帝国政府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Japanese government|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大日本帝国|1=Empire of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q188712}}.
== List ==
# {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|1=Senior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Junior First Rank|ja|従一位|1=Junior First Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior First Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior First Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Senior Second Rank|ja|正二位|1=Senior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Second Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Junior Second Rank|ja|従二位|1=Junior Second Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Second Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Second Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Third Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Junior Third Rank|ja|従三位|1=Junior Third Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Third Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank|ja|正四位|1=Senior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fourth Rank|qq=}} 
# {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|ja|従四位|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Senior Fifth Rank|ja|正五位|1=Senior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Senior Sixth Rank|ja|正六位|1=Senior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Sixth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Junior Sixth Rank|ja|従六位|1=Junior Sixth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Senior Seventh Rank|ja|正七位|1=Senior Seventh Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Seventh Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Seventh Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Junior Seventh Rank|ja|従七位|1=Junior Seventh Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Seventh Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Seventh Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Senior Eighth Rank|ja|正八位|1=Senior Eighth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Eighth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Eighth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Junior Eighth Rank|ja|従八位|1=Junior Eighth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Eighth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Eighth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Senior Ninth Rank|ja|正九位|1=Senior Ninth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Ninth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Ninth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Junior Ninth Rank|ja|従九位|1=Junior Ninth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Ninth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Ninth Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Senior Initial Rank|ja|大初位|1=Senior Initial Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Initial Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Initial Rank|qq=}}
# {{ill|Junior Initial Rank|ja|少初位|1=Junior Initial Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|qq=}}
== Other Nations’ Systems ==
*{{ill|Nine-rank system|en|Nine-rank system|qid=Q1151136|lt=Nine-Rank system|1=Nine-rank system}} (China)
*{{ill|Gwageo|ja|科久|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gwageo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Korea)
*{{ill|Bone-rank system|en|Bone-rank system|qid=Q698087|lt=Bone Rank system|1=Bone-rank system}} (Silla)
